claimed_contribution: The paper jointly models hotel quality choice at purchase and post-stay satisfaction rather than treating them as separate outcomes.
It introduces and empirically evaluates three purchase-time circumstantial variables (lead time, travel distance, and business-hours booking) as drivers of both choice and satisfaction.
It embeds these variables in a formal two-stage economic model with correlated choice and satisfaction components, showing the value of adding circumstantial factors to prediction.
context: The setting is online hotel booking on a discount travel website that shows star rating and price before purchase but reveals the hotel name after payment. Customers prepay at reservation and then receive a post-checkout satisfaction survey; the analysis focuses on leisure trips to 200 U.S. destination cities.
design_and_data: The authors use field data from an online travel site covering reservations from January 2008 to October 2009. From 10,193 initial customers, they construct a leisure-trip sample of 4,582 consumers making 5,093 trips, with 120,758 hotel alternatives observed across those trips. They model behavior in two stages: a nested logit for hotel choice and an ordered satisfaction model, jointly estimated with correlated errors using a flexible bivariate logistic structure and hierarchical Bayes. They estimate on 3,382 trips and assess predictive performance on a 1,711-trip holdout sample.
focal_constructs: time gap between purchase and consumption
time of purchase (business vs nonbusiness hours)
main_findings: Consumers who book earlier, travel farther, and book during business hours are more likely to choose higher-star hotels.
Consumers who travel farther and consumers who book during business hours are less satisfied with their hotel stay.
Earlier booking is associated with higher satisfaction overall, and the estimates indicate a significant prospective-accounting effect that can outweigh the opposing higher-expected-utility effect.
one_line_summary: Using online hotel reservation and survey data, the study shows that booking lead time, travel distance, and business-hour booking jointly influence both hotel quality choice and post-stay satisfaction.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior research that has examined purchase choice and satisfaction in separate streams by modeling both decisions jointly in one empirical framework. It also links purchase-time circumstantial variables, beyond standard expectation-based antecedents, to subsequent satisfaction judgments.
research_gap: Prior research has largely treated purchase decisions and satisfaction judgments separately, and the satisfaction literature had not clearly tied purchase-time circumstances to later satisfaction assessments. The authors also argue that the three focal circumstantial variables had not been empirically examined together in this context.
research_question: Do purchase-time circumstances - booking lead time, traveler-to-destination distance, and whether booking occurs during business hours - affect both the quality level of hotel chosen and post-stay satisfaction? Can a joint two-stage model quantify these effects while accounting for dependence between choice and satisfaction and the opposing expectation versus prospective-accounting mechanisms?
